one_line_takeaway: A 5M-parameter bidirectional GRU trained on nothing but a model's own top-20 token log-probability trajectories detects hallucinations better than a 30x-larger fine-tuned ModernBERT encoder (Lettuce) on 7/10 HUB clusters, but the detector's learned calibration-bias signal is model-specific and degrades sharply when applied to a different LLM's log-probs.
key_finding: On HUB test clusters (Table 2, macro-F1, HALT-L vs. Llama 3.1-8B log-probs): HALT-L wins on 7 of 10 clusters and achieves the best Overall (67.01) and Average (63.03) scores, versus Lettuce's Overall 64.00 / Average 48.50 and the best aggregate-statistic baseline ΔHdec's Overall 48.03 / Average 43.41. HALT-L particularly dominates Algorithmic (76.80 vs. Lettuce's 24.24), World Knowledge (76.92 vs. 44.51), and Symbolic (65.40 vs. 33.36) clusters; Lettuce remains stronger on knowledge/reference-heavy clusters it more closely resembles in training distribution, Data2Text (83.38 vs. HALT-L's 72.89) and QA (77.30 vs. 74.07). HALT-Q (trained on Qwen 2.5-7B, hyperparameters transferred untuned from HALT-L) is generally weaker (Overall 62.74, Average 55.60) but still beats Lettuce's average and wins on Summarization (70.75 vs. HALT-L's 66.93). On external gold-standard sets (Table 3): on FAVA-Annotations, HALT-L achieves Macro-F1 61.57, clearly ahead of Lettuce's 40.33 and all aggregate-statistics baselines (best is ΔHdec at 44.24), and remains competitive despite the FAVA model being ~1400x larger. On RAGTruth, Lettuce wins outright (Macro-F1 80.00 vs. HALT-L's 65.70), attributed by the authors to Lettuce having been trained directly on RAGTruth data. Table 4 (HUB transferability, average AUROC/Accuracy/Macro-F1 across clusters) shows HALT-L (70.02/67.02/63.04) and HALT-Q (61.11/59.65/55.60) both clearly outperform Lettuce (59.05/61.82/48.50) and all trivial baselines (Constant-Positive/Negative ~32 Macro-F1, Random ~48-51 Macro-F1). Critically, cross-model transfer degrades performance sharply: HALT-L applied to Qwen log-probs drops to 63.99/58.61/54.20, and HALT-Q applied to LLaMA log-probs drops to 55.24/52.62/50.01 -- both close to the Lettuce baseline and confirming the paper's own Hypothesis 3 that calibration-bias detectors do not transfer reliably across different LLM architectures even though they generalize well across tasks within the same model (Hypothesis 4).
core_contribution: The paper introduces HALT (Hallucination Assessment via Log-probs as Time series), a black-box hallucination detector that treats the sequence of top-20 token log-probabilities from an LLM's generation as a time series and classifies it with a lightweight bidirectional GRU (5M parameters) combined with hand-engineered entropy/calibration features (average log-prob, rank proxy of the selected token, overall entropy over the top-k distribution, alternatives-only entropy, and the temporal delta of a binary decision entropy). Unlike white-box detectors it needs no hidden states or attention maps, and unlike black-box text-based detectors it does not read the generated surface text or call external verifiers/retrievers -- it only needs the log-probability stream, which some proprietary APIs expose. The paper also introduces its own benchmark, HUB (Hallucination detection Unified Benchmark), which aggregates and re-splits prior datasets (HaluEval, FAVA, RAGTruth, CriticBench) into 10 task clusters spanning five 'reasoning' capabilities (Algorithmic, Commonsense, Mathematical, Symbolic, Code Generation) and five 'general-purpose' capabilities (Chat, Data-to-Text, QA, Summarization, World Knowledge), explicitly extending the notion of hallucination to include logical/reasoning errors, not just factual ones. Two trained variants are released: HALT-L (trained on Llama 3.1-8B log-probs) and HALT-Q (trained on Qwen 2.5-7B log-probs).
threat_model: N/A -- this is not a security paper. There is no adversary, attacker capability, or threat model in the paper. The problem addressed is model reliability/calibration (detecting when an LLM's own generation is factually or logically unfaithful), not an adversarial security scenario. The paper's own Impact Statement frames the contribution as 'efficient and privacy-preserving hallucination detection' for trustworthy-AI purposes, explicitly scoped to detection rather than diagnosing root causes.
limitations: The paper does not contain a dedicated 'Limitations' section (it has an Impact Statement in its place), but the results themselves surface the key limitation the authors explicitly discuss: cross-model transfer failure (Hypothesis 3, confirmed in Table 4) means a detector trained on one LLM's log-probabilities cannot be reused on another LLM without substantial performance loss, so each new target model in principle requires its own trained HALT variant with access to its log-probs. HALT-Q was only ever tuned via hyperparameters inherited from HALT-L (never independently tuned), which the authors note 'partly explains its lower overall performance,' making the HALT-L vs. HALT-Q comparison not fully apples-to-apples. The method still requires log-probability access (a lighter but nonzero form of model access) rather than working from text alone, so it is inapplicable to APIs that expose neither raw text likelihoods nor scores. On RAGTruth, HALT-L is outperformed by Lettuce, which the authors attribute to Lettuce's in-distribution training advantage, indicating HALT's edge is not universal across all reference-based benchmarks. The Impact Statement explicitly scopes the paper to detection, not diagnosis of hallucination root causes or interpretability of why particular log-prob trajectories signal hallucination.

datasets_used: HUB (paper's own benchmark, built from CriticBench clusters plus HaluEval, FAVA Annotations, and RAGTruth): 10 task clusters -- Algorithmic Reasoning, Chat, Code Generation, Commonsense Reasoning, Data-to-Text, Mathematical Reasoning, Question Answering, Summarization, Symbolic Reasoning, World Knowledge; overall train/val/test sizes 60,008 / 7,342 / 8,114 with hallucination ratios ~50.00% / 48.31% / 47.27%
HaluEval (Li et al., 2023) -- balanced 500-example subset held out for testing
FAVA Annotations (Mishra et al., 2024) -- human-annotated gold-standard external test set
RAGTruth (Niu et al., 2024) -- test split used as external held-out set
CriticBench (Lan et al., 2024) -- source of reasoning-task clusters incorporated into HUB
quantitative_results: HUB Overall macro-F1 (Table 2): HALT-L 67.01, HALT-Q 62.74, Lettuce 64.00, best aggregate baseline (ΔHdec) 48.03
HUB Average macro-F1 (Table 2, across 10 clusters): HALT-L 63.03, HALT-Q 55.60, Lettuce 48.50, ΔHdec 43.41, PPL 34.08
HALT-L wins on 7 of 10 HUB clusters (per paper's own text)
FAVA-Annotations Macro-F1 (Table 3): HALT-L 61.57 vs. Lettuce 40.33 vs. best aggregate stat ΔHdec 44.24
RAGTruth Macro-F1 (Table 3): Lettuce 80.00 vs. HALT-L 65.70 vs. best aggregate stat ΔHdec 47.30
HUB transferability average AUROC/Accuracy/Macro-F1 (Table 4): HALT-L 70.02/67.02/63.04, HALT-Q 61.11/59.65/55.60, Lettuce 59.05/61.82/48.50
Cross-model transfer degradation (Table 4): HALT-L on Qwen log-probs 63.99/58.61/54.20; HALT-Q on LLaMA log-probs 55.24/52.62/50.01
Trivial baselines (Table 4): Constant-Positive Macro-F1 32.17, Constant-Negative 32.60, Random Baseline AUROC 49.67 / Macro-F1 47.93

Model size and speed claims (Abstract/Intro): HALT is a 5M-parameter GRU, 30x smaller than Lettuce's ModernBERT-base encoder, and achieves a 60x inference speedup over Lettuce on HUB; HALT-L is also described as ~1400x smaller than the FAVA model (fine-tuned LLaMA-7B + retriever)
baselines_compared: Lettuce (fine-tuned ModernBERT-base span-level hallucination detector, black-box/text-based)
Aggregate log-prob statistics: PPL, Hoverall, Halts, ΔHdec (non-learned scores derived from the same log-prob features HALT uses)
